<result>This confirms `connections.py`'s test-connection path is clean — the adapter exception text (which could carry a DSN/credential fragment) is never logged or returned to the client, only `type(exc).__name__` is logged and a fixed message returned. Good.

I have everything needed for the report.

## Security spot check — remaining API/MCP surface (2026-07-05 → 2026-07-12 batch)

**connections.py** — clean. All routes `get_current_user`-gated (workspace-wide resource, consistent with the existing connection model — no suite-scoping applies here). Responses never carry secrets (`has_secret` only). `test_connection`/`reauth_connection` delegate to `connection_service`, which explicitly avoids echoing the adapter exception (DSN/credential fragments) to client or logs — only `type(exc).__name__` is logged, a fixed `ConnectionTestFailedError` message returned (`backend/app/services/connection_service.py:463-474`). This is the #536-lesson pattern applied correctly.

**notifications.py** — clean. `require_permission(minimum="view"/"edit")` on every route; webhook URLs write-only through `SecretStore`, never returned (`has_webhook`/`has_slack_webhook` only).

**probe.py** — clean. Unchanged demonstrator endpoint, `get_current_user`-gated, no new surface.

**runs.py** — clean, including the new comparison-report download route. Suite-scoped `require_permission(view)` on `get_run`/`get_run_progress`/`cancel_run`(edit)/`download_comparison_report`; 404-before-403 existence hiding preserved. Sample redaction is field-built (never `model_validate` passthrough) via `redact_sample_failures`. The new `download_comparison_report` (lines 358-393) re-derives the report from the already-redacted `sample_failures` at request time — never a stored unredacted file — and gates identically to run-detail (view permission + 404 checks on both run and result).

**schedules.py** — clean. All routes `get_current_user`-gated with service-level suite-ownership checks (`user_id=`/`include_all=is_workspace_admin`); Pydantic length/format constraints on cron/timezone.

**suites.py** — clean. Consistent `require_permission` ladder (view/edit/admin) across CRUD, run-trigger, export/import, profiler, column-policy routes; all request bodies use bounded `Field` constraints; the new comparison-related `SourceConnectionRef`/`CheckDocument.source_connection` fields are simple bounded strings, no raw dict passthrough.

**rate_limit.py (#783)** — clean. Fails open on any store exception (`RedisStore.incr_windows` catches `Exception` broadly → `None` → allow). The 429 response body (`error_envelope`) carries only `retry_after_seconds`; no internal state (counts, keys, Redis details) leaks into the response or is logged (only `key.split(":",1)[0]` — kind, never value). Dev-bypass scoping is correct and prod-safe: `docker-compose.yml` defaults `RATE_LIMIT_ENABLED` to `false` for local dev only, `ci.yml` hardcodes `"false"` for CI/test, and the prod template `.env.app.example` sets `true`; Terraform (`containerapps.tf`) does not override the flag at all, so prod falls back to the `Settings` default `rate_limit_enabled: bool = True` — there is no path by which the dev/CI bypass reaches a real deployment.

**comparison_run.py / comparison_report.py / datasources/comparison.py / dataset_reader.py** — clean. `dataset_reader.py`'s SQL path re-validates any `query` spec through `validate_query` (ADR 0019 read-only/single-statement) a second time at read time (defense in depth beyond author-time validation) before string-interpolating it into COUNT/LIMIT wrappers; table-addressed reads use parameterized `sqlalchemy.select`. Row caps are enforced fail-fast (COUNT preflight where available, always a post-read length check) raising `DatasetTooLargeError` rather than silently truncating. `comparison_run.py` classifies unexpected exceptions through `classify_failure_reason` (never raw exception text, which could carry DSN/credential fragments) and logs server-side only; `DataQError` messages are redaction-safe by construction and surface verbatim. Report building (`comparison_report.py`) neutralizes CSV/XLSX formula-injection leaders on every cell. Sample/diff rows: confirmed `run_service.redact_sample_failures` has an explicit `_COMPARISON_SAMPLE_KEYS` branch (`_redact_comparison_row`) that suffix-strips `<col>_src`/`<col>_tgt` before policy/classifier matching, so comparison buckets get the same column-aware PII redaction as expectation-check samples — this was verified, not assumed.

**mcp/server.py** — one real finding, otherwise clean:
- Authz/redaction parity holds for all 8 tools: same `require_permission` calls at the same minimum levels as the REST equivalents (`get_suite_results`/`get_run_status` = view, `trigger_suite_run`/`create_check`/`profile_column` = edit), same `redact_sample_failures` call for sample rows, same NUL-byte rejection (`_reject_nul`) as the REST `ApiModel` boundary. `get_adf_pipeline_status` is actually stricter than the REST `/pipeline_runs` (it filters the correlated DQ run by `accessible` suite ids before exposing it).
- **Finding — input validation gap, `create_check` tool (`backend/app/mcp/server.py:346-398`):** the MCP tool's `name`/`expectation_type` parameters are plain `str` with no length bound, whereas the REST `CheckCreate` model caps them at `max_length=256`/`128` (`backend/app/api/v1/checks.py:36,40`). `check_service.create_check` itself enforces no string-length limit either (confirmed by reading `validate_kind`/`validate_monitor_check`/`validate_comparison_check`), so an MCP caller can currently store an arbitrarily large `name`/`expectation_type` that the REST path would reject with a 422. Low severity (authenticated, edit-permission-gated, no injection — just an unbounded-size / minor-DoS/data-quality gap), but it is a real REST-vs-MCP inconsistency worth a backlog issue rather than silent acceptance.

**Webhook surfaces** — not applicable this window, per the brief (no orchestration webhook files changed).</result>

content.text:
## Weekly Security Scan — 2026-07-05 → 2026-07-12

| # | Check | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dependabot alerts (open) | ✅ Clean — 0 open alerts |
| 2 | GitHub secret-scanning alerts | ✅ Clean — 0 open alerts |
| 3 | Local `pip-audit -r backend/requirements-dev.txt` | ✅ Clean — no known vulnerabilities |
| 3 | Local `pnpm audit --audit-level=high` | ✅ Clean — no known vulnerabilities |
| 4 | Full-tree `betterleaks` sweep | ✅ Clean — passed |
| 5 | OWASP spot-check on changed endpoints | 🔴 1 finding (low severity) |
| 6 | Key Vault access audit | ✅ Clean |
| 7 | Credential-rotation register | 🔴 1 flag (30d PAT approaching expiry) |

### Details

**Step 5 — OWASP spot-check** (surface: `assets.py`, `checks.py`, `connections.py`, `incidents.py`, `notifications.py`, `probe.py`, `runs.py`, `schedules.py`, `suites.py`, `mcp/server.py`, plus the new comparison pipeline and `rate_limit.py`)

All authz (grant-derived 404-no-leak on assets/incidents, `require_permission` ladders elsewhere), input validation, error-envelope hygiene (the #536 lesson holds — `connection_service` never echoes adapter exception text), PII redaction (comparison diff rows go through a dedicated `_redact_comparison_row` branch), and rate-limiter fail-open/no-leak behavior checked out clean.

One finding:
- **`backend/app/mcp/server.py:346-398`, `create_check` tool** — `name`/`expectation_type` params are unbounded `str`, while the REST `CheckCreate` model caps them at 256/128 chars (`backend/app/api/v1/checks.py:36,40`), and `check_service` itself enforces no length limit. An MCP caller (edit-permission-gated, authenticated) can store arbitrarily large strings the REST path would reject with a 422. Low severity — inconsistency/minor DoS-via-storage-bloat, not an injection or authz bypass. This is a **non-sensitive hardening item**, so per the skill's routing rule it belongs in a normal GitHub issue (not a security advisory) — say the word and I'll file it via `/gh-issue-from-finding`.

**Step 6 — Key Vault (`dataq-app-kv-aw6laj`)**: 3 role holders, all expected — your account (Secrets Officer), `dataq-terraform-sp` (Secrets User, deploy CI), `dataq-app-id` (custom least-priv RW role, the app's UAMI — matches #622). No unexpected principals.

**Step 7 — Credential rotation**:
- Databricks PAT (`databricks-token-harness`) — last updated 2026-07-02, consistent with the required post-#536/#538 rotation. ✅ confirmed happened.
- ADLS SAS — expires 2027-06-28. Clean.
- Snowflake per-connection secrets — updated 2026-06-28 (orders/payments) / 2026-07-05 (retail), well inside the 90-day window.
- `dataq-pat-w1-member` (90d, minted 2026-07-05) — 83 days remaining. Clean.
- 🔴 **`dataq-pat-w1-admin`** (30d lifetime, minted 2026-07-05) — **23 days remaining, expires ~2026-08-04.** Within the 30-day flag threshold; no action needed yet, but plan the re-mint (recovery path per memory: re-mint + KV update).
- Webhook secrets (ADF/Airflow/dbt) — event-driven rotation only, no suspected exposure. Clean.

### Verdict: **2 findings — action needed (both low-severity, non-blocking)**
1. File a normal issue for the MCP `create_check` length-bound gap (your call whether to do it now).
2. No action required yet on the admin PAT, just keep it on your radar for a re-mint before 2026-08-04.

content.content:
openlineage import (the dark path). Construction is fail-open **and does not
    latch**: a bad transport config logs a warning once (``_warned``) and returns
    ``None`` without caching it, so a transient failure self-heals on the next call
    rather than going dark for the process lifetime.
    """
    global _client, _client_configured, _warned
    if _client_configured:
        return _client
    with _client_lock:
        if _client_configured:
            return _client
        if not is_emission_configured():
            _client = None
            _client_configured = True  # dark path latches (cheap, stable)
            return None
        try:
            client = _build_client()
        except Exception:
            # Bad transport config (bad URL scheme, unreadable config file, …) must
            # not fail a run. Warn once, and DON'T latch — retry on the next call.
            if not _warned:
                log.warning("openlineage_client_init_failed", exc_info=True)
                _warned = True
            return None
        _client = client
        _client_configured = True
        log.info("openlineage_client_initialized")
        return client


def reset_openlineage_client_cache() -> None:
    """Reset the cached client so the next call re-evaluates config.

    Used by the ``worker_process_init`` fork-safety handler (so a prefork child
    never inherits a parent-built client) and by tests.
    """

"""The fail-open OpenLineage choke point the worker calls (ADR 0034, #758).

``emit_run_lineage_start`` / ``emit_run_lineage_terminal`` load a run's graph, build
the event, and emit it. Both are **best-effort and never raise** — lineage emission
is a browse/reason convenience layered over the execution model, so a dead or slow
OpenLineage receiver must never fail or roll back an already-persisted run. When
emission is unconfigured the client is ``None`` and each returns ``False``
immediately, before touching the session (zero queries on the dark path). Precedent:
``alerting.dispatch.publish_run_outcome``.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import uuid
from collections.abc import Callable
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING

from sqlalchemy.orm import Session

from backend.app.core.logging import get_logger
from backend.app.db.models import Asset, Run, Suite
from backend.app.lineage import emitter
from backend.app.services import check_service, run_service

if TYPE_CHECKING:
    from openlineage.client.event_v2 import RunEvent

log = get_logger(__name__)


def _emit(
    session: Session,
    *,
    run_id: uuid.UUID,
    event: str,

        def close(self) -> None:
            pass

    monkeypatch.setattr(lineage_pull, "get_lineage_provider", lambda: object())
    monkeypatch.setattr(tasks, "get_session", lambda: _Session())
    monkeypatch.setattr(lineage_pull, "refresh_pulled_edges", lambda *a, **k: None)

    # refresh returning None (fail-soft skip) surfaces as 0, not None
    assert tasks.refresh_lineage_pull() == 0

@mcp.tool
def create_check(
    suite_id: str,
    name: str,
    expectation_type: str,
    config: dict[str, Any] | None = None,
    kind: str = "expectation",
    warn_threshold: float | None = None,
    fail_threshold: float | None = None,
    critical_threshold: float | None = None,
    source_connection_id: str | None = None,
) -> dict[str, Any]:
    """Add a new check (a Great Expectations expectation) to a suite.

    Use this for 'add a null check on email to the customer suite'. ``name`` is a
    human label; ``expectation_type`` is a GX expectation (e.g.
    ``expect_column_values_to_not_be_null``); ``config`` carries its arguments
    (e.g. ``{"column": "email"}``). Optional warn/fail/critical thresholds band
    the result severity. For a cross-dataset reconciliation check use
    ``kind="comparison"`` with ``expectation_type="comparison:records"``,
    ``source_connection_id`` (the baseline connection to compare against) and a
    config carrying ``source`` (the baseline dataset spec) + ``keys`` (join key
    columns). Requires edit access. Returns the created check's id.
    """
    sid = _parse_uuid(suite_id, field="suite_id")
    _reject_nul(name=name, expectation_type=expectation_type, kind=kind, config=config or {})
    with _ctx() as (session, user), _service_errors():
        require_permission(session, sid, user.id, minimum="edit")
        check = check_service.create_check(
            session,
            suite_id=sid,
            name=name,
            kind=kind,
            expectation_type=expectation_type,
            config=config or {},
            source_connection_id=(
                _parse_uuid(source_connection_id, field="source_connection_id")
                if source_connection_id is not None
                else None
            ),
            warn_threshold=Decimal(str(warn_threshold)) if warn_threshold is not None else None,
            fail_threshold=Decimal(str(fail_threshold)) if fail_threshold is not None else None,
            critical_threshold=(
                Decimal(str(critical_threshold)) if critical_threshold is not None else None
            ),
            actor_id=user.id,
        )
        return {
            "id": str(check.id),
            "suite_id": suite_id,
            "name": check.name,
            "expectation_type": check.expectation_type,
        }

65	# 0012, pulled into v1 per the 2026-06-29 amendment), and `comparison` (ADR
66	# 0015 — authorable now, runnable when the #794 runner lands; until then a
67	# comparison check yields an `error` result, see run_service). The remaining
68	# reserved kinds (schema_drift / anomaly) have no model yet, so CRUD refuses.
69	_V1_SUPPORTED_KINDS = {"expectation", *MONITOR_KINDS, COMPARISON_KIND}
70	
71	# Canonical expectation_types for a comparison check (mirrors `monitor:<kind>`).
72	# `comparison:records` = row grain; `comparison:columns` = FDC's per-column
73	# value grain (#799).
74	COMPARISON_EXPECTATION_TYPE = "comparison:records"
75	COMPARISON_EXPECTATION_TYPES = ("comparison:records", "comparison:columns")
76	
77	# Datasources whose runner implements `run_monitors` (a `MonitorRunner`) — the
78	# author-time gate for freshness/volume checks. The SQL datasources compute the
79	# aggregate in-warehouse; Iceberg computes it natively (`scan().count()` / a column
80	# MAX, ADR 0030). This is broader than `SQL_QUERYABLE_TYPES` (which gates *custom
81	# SQL* — Iceberg is a native DataFrame read, not SQL-queryable), so the two stay
82	# distinct. Kept in sync with the run path's `isinstance(runner, MonitorRunner)`.
83	MONITOR_CAPABLE_TYPES = frozenset({*SQL_QUERYABLE_TYPES, "iceberg"})
84	
85

87	    status_code = 404
88	    code = "check_not_found"
89

92	    status_code = 422
93	    code = "check_config_invalid"

95	
96	# The unique-constraint name on `check_versions(check_id, version_no)` — the
97	# concurrency backstop a racing double-edit trips. Matched against the DB error
98	# so only that collision becomes a 409 (see `update_check`).
99	_VERSION_UNIQUE_CONSTRAINT = "uq_check_versions_check_version"
100	
101	
102	class CheckEditConflictError(DataQError):
103	    # A concurrent edit of the same check raced on the `(check_id, version_no)`
104	    # snapshot backstop (#309-adjacent C3): a benign write-write collision, so 409
105	    # (reload + retry) — not an unhandled 500. read-modify-write is only as safe as
106	    # its unique constraint (no row-locking on the check-then-write today).
107	    status_code = 409
108	    code = "check_edit_conflict"
109

111	def _connection_type(session: Session, suite: Suite) -> str:
112	    """The datasource type of the suite's connection — for custom-SQL gating.
113	
114	    The suite's `connection_id` FK is NOT NULL, so the connection always exists.
115	    """
116	    connection = session.get(Connection, suite.connection_id)
117	    assert connection is not None
118	    return connection.type

122	    """Reject an unsupported check kind (422). Shared by CRUD and suite import.
123	
124	    v1 supports `expectation` + the freshness/volume monitor kinds; the remaining
125	    reserved kinds (ADR 0012) have no runner yet, so authoring one is refused."""
126	    if kind not in _V1_SUPPORTED_KINDS:

129	            detail={"kind": kind, "supported": sorted(_V1_SUPPORTED_KINDS)},
130	        )
131	
132	
133	def validate_monitor_check(
134	    kind: str,
135	    config: dict[str, Any],
136	    *,
137	    expectation_type: str,
138	    connection_type: str,
139	    fail_threshold: Decimal | None,
140	    critical_threshold: Decimal | None,
141	) -> None:
142	    """Validate a freshness/volume monitor check at author time (create/update).
143	
144	    Four gates, each a 422:
145	    1. **Monitor-capable datasource only** — a monitor needs a datasource whose runner
146	       implements `run_monitors` (`MONITOR_CAPABLE_TYPES`: the SQL datasources compute
147	       the aggregate in-warehouse; Iceberg computes it natively). A monitor on a
148	       flat-file suite would only fail at run time (its runner has no `run_monitors`),
149	       so reject it up front. Broader than custom-SQL's `SQL_QUERYABLE_TYPES` — Iceberg
150	       supports monitors but is not SQL-queryable.
151	    2. **expectation_type matches the kind** — a monitor's type is the canonical
152	       ``monitor:<kind>``. The run path keys off `kind`, so a mismatched/junk type
153	       would still execute but mislabel every result row (and could smuggle a
154	       custom-SQL type past its guardrails) — keep the stored row self-consistent.
155	    3. **Config shape** — a valid `column` (freshness) or `min_rows`/`max_rows` range
156	       (volume), via the shared `monitors.validate_monitor_config`.
157	    4. **Freshness needs a positive threshold** — freshness has no in-config bound, so
158	       without a fail/critical age threshold it would always resolve `pass` no matter

160	       threshold is the inverse footgun (always fail). Require a positive fail-or-
161	       critical threshold so a freshness check bands meaningfully.
162	    """

from backend.app.services.suite_service import get_suite

log = get_logger(__name__)

# Authorable kinds: GX expectations, the freshness/volume monitor kinds (ADR
# 0012, pulled into v1 per the 2026-06-29 amendment), and `comparison` (ADR
# 0015 — authorable now, runnable when the #794 runner lands; until then a
# comparison check yields an `error` result, see run_service). The remaining
# reserved kinds (schema_drift / anomaly) have no model yet, so CRUD refuses.
_V1_SUPPORTED_KINDS = {"expectation", *MONITOR_KINDS, COMPARISON_KIND}

# Canonical expectation_types for a comparison check (mirrors `monitor:<kind>`).
# `comparison:records` = row grain; `comparison:columns` = FDC's per-column
# value grain (#799).
COMPARISON_EXPECTATION_TYPE = "comparison:records"
COMPARISON_EXPECTATION_TYPES = ("comparison:records", "comparison:columns")

# Datasources whose runner implements `run_monitors` (a `MonitorRunner`) — the
# author-time gate for freshness/volume checks. The SQL datasources compute the
# aggregate in-warehouse; Iceberg computes it natively (`scan().count()` / a column
# MAX, ADR 0030). This is broader than `SQL_QUERYABLE_TYPES` (which gates *custom
# SQL* — Iceberg is a native DataFrame read, not SQL-queryable), so the two stay
# distinct. Kept in sync with the run path's `isinstance(runner, MonitorRunner)`.
MONITOR_CAPABLE_TYPES = frozenset({*SQL_QUERYABLE_TYPES, "iceberg"})


class CheckNotFoundError(DataQError):
    status_code = 404
    code = "check_not_found"


class CheckConfigInvalidError(DataQError):
    status_code = 422
    code = "check_config_invalid"


# The unique-constraint name on `check_versions(check_id, version_no)` — the
# concurrency backstop a racing double-edit trips. Matched against the DB error
# so only that collision becomes a 409 (see `update_check`).
_VERSION_UNIQUE_CONSTRAINT = "uq_check_versions_check_version"


class CheckEditConflictError(DataQError):
    # A concurrent edit of the same check raced on the `(check_id, version_no)`
    # snapshot backstop (#309-adjacent C3): a benign write-write collision, so 409
    # (reload + retry) — not an unhandled 500. read-modify-write is only as safe as
    # its unique constraint (no row-locking on the check-then-write today).
    status_code = 409
    code = "check_edit_conflict"


def _connection_type(session: Session, suite: Suite) -> str:
    """The datasource type of the suite's connection — for custom-SQL gating.

    The suite's `connection_id` FK is NOT NULL, so the connection always exists.
    """
    connection = session.get(Connection, suite.connection_id)
    assert connection is not None
    return connection.type


def validate_kind(kind: str) -> None:
    """Reject an unsupported check kind (422). Shared by CRUD and suite import.

    v1 supports `expectation` + the freshness/volume monitor kinds; the remaining
    reserved kinds (ADR 0012) have no runner yet, so authoring one is refused."""
    if kind not in _V1_SUPPORTED_KINDS:
        raise CheckConfigInvalidError(
            f"check kind {kind!r} is not supported in v1",
            detail={"kind": kind, "supported": sorted(_V1_SUPPORTED_KINDS)},
        )


def validate_monitor_check(
    kind: str,
    config: dict[str, Any],
    *,
    expectation_type: str,
    connection_type: str,
    fail_threshold: Decimal | None,
    critical_threshold: Decimal | None,
) -> None:
    """Validate a freshness/volume monitor check at author time (create/update).

    Four gates, each a 422:
    1. **Monitor-capable datasource only** — a monitor needs a datasource whose runner
       implements `run_monitors` (`MONITOR_CAPABLE_TYPES`: the SQL datasources compute
       the aggregate in-warehouse; Iceberg computes it natively). A monitor on a
       flat-file suite would only fail at run time (its runner has no `run_monitors`),
       so reject it up front. Broader than custom-SQL's `SQL_QUERYABLE_TYPES` — Iceberg
       supports monitors but is not SQL-queryable.
    2. **expectation_type matches the kind** — a monitor's type is the canonical
       ``monitor:<kind>``. The run path keys off `kind`, so a mismatched/junk type
       would still execute but mislabel every result row (and could smuggle a
       custom-SQL type past its guardrails) — keep the stored row self-consistent.
    3. **Config shape** — a valid `column` (freshness) or `min_rows`/`max_rows` range
       (volume), via the shared `monitors.validate_monitor_config`.
    4. **Freshness needs a positive threshold** — freshness has no in-config bound, so
       without a fail/critical age threshold it would always resolve `pass` no matter
       how stale (the silent-green footgun flagged in the #426 review); a *zero*
       threshold is the inverse footgun (always fail). Require a positive fail-or-
       critical threshold so a freshness check bands meaningfully.
    """
    if connection_type not in MONITOR_CAPABLE_TYPES:
        raise CheckConfigInvalidError(
            f"{kind} monitor checks require a monitor-capable datasource, not {connection_type!r}",
            detail={
                "connection_type": connection_type,
                "supported": sorted(MONITOR_CAPABLE_TYPES),
            },
        )
    expected_type = monitor_expectation_type(kind)
    if expectation_type != expected_type:
        raise CheckConfigInvalidError(
            f"a {kind} monitor's expectation_type must be {expected_type!r}, not "
            f"{expectation_type!r}",
            detail={"kind": kind, "expectation_type": expectation_type},
        )
    try:
        validate_monitor_config(kind, config)
    except MonitorConfigError as exc:
        raise CheckConfigInvalidError(str(exc), detail={"kind": kind, "config": config}) from exc
    if kind == FRESHNESS and not _has_positive_threshold(fail_threshold, critical_threshold):
        raise CheckConfigInvalidError(
            "a freshness monitor needs a positive fail or critical age threshold (hours) — "
            "without one it can never fail (no threshold) or always fails (zero)",
            detail={"kind": kind},
        )


def _has_positive_threshold(fail: Decimal | None, critical: Decimal | None) -> bool:
    """Whether a fail or critical threshold is set to a positive value."""
    return (fail is not None and fail > 0) or (critical is not None and critical > 0)

    """
    if not isinstance(keys, list) or not keys:
        raise CheckConfigInvalidError(
            "a comparison check needs config.keys — a non-empty list of join key columns",
            detail={"field": "config.keys"},

def create_check(
    session: Session,
    *,
    suite_id: uuid.UUID,
    name: str,
    kind: str,
    expectation_type: str,
    config: dict[str, Any],
    warn_threshold: Decimal | None,
    fail_threshold: Decimal | None,
    critical_threshold: Decimal | None,
    source_connection_id: uuid.UUID | None = None,
    actor_id: uuid.UUID | None = None,
) -> Check:
    """Create a check in a suite, recording its first version (#280).

    Raises `SuiteNotFoundError` (404) if the suite does not exist, or
    `CheckConfigInvalidError` (422) for an unsupported kind.
    """
    suite = get_suite(session, suite_id)  # 404 if the suite is missing
    validate_kind(kind)
    if kind != COMPARISON_KIND and source_connection_id is not None:
        raise CheckConfigInvalidError(
            "only comparison checks carry a source connection (ADR 0015)",
            detail={"kind": kind, "field": "source_connection_id"},
        )
    if kind in MONITOR_KINDS:
        validate_monitor_check(
            kind,
            config,
            expectation_type=expectation_type,
            connection_type=_connection_type(session, suite),
            fail_threshold=fail_threshold,
            critical_threshold=critical_threshold,
        )
    elif kind == COMPARISON_KIND:
        validate_comparison_check(
            session,
            config=config,
            expectation_type=expectation_type,
            source_connection_id=source_connection_id,
            suite_connection_type=_connection_type(session, suite),
        )
    elif is_custom_sql(expectation_type):
        validate_custom_sql_check(
            expectation_type=expectation_type,
            config=config,
            connection_type=_connection_type(session, suite),
        )
    else:
        validate_expectation_check(expectation_type, config)

    check = Check(
        suite_id=suite_id,
        name=name,
        kind=kind,
        expectation_type=expectation_type,
        source_connection_id=source_connection_id,
        config=config,
        warn_threshold=warn_threshold,
        fail_threshold=fail_threshold,
        critical_threshold=critical_threshold,
    )
    session.add(check)
    session.flush()  # assign check.id so the v1 snapshot can reference it
    record_check_version(session, check, actor_id=actor_id)
    session.commit()
    session.refresh(check)
    log.info("check_created", check_id=str(check.id), suite_id=str(suite_id))
    return check


def list_checks(session: Session, suite_id: uuid.UUID) -> list[Check]:
    """List a suite's checks (404 if the suite does not exist)."""
    get_suite(session, suite_id)
    stmt = select(Check).where(Check.suite_id == suite_id).order_by(Check.created_at)
    return list(session.scalars(stmt))


def get_check(session: Session, suite_id: uuid.UUID, check_id: uuid.UUID) -> Check:
    """Fetch a check, enforcing that it belongs to `suite_id` (else 404)."""
    check = session.get(Check, check_id)
    if check is None or check.suite_id != suite_id:
        raise CheckNotFoundError(
            "check not found",
            detail={"suite_id": str(suite_id), "check_id": str(check_id)},
        )
    return check


def update_check(
    session: Session,
    suite_id: uuid.UUID,
    check_id: uuid.UUID,
    *,
    name: str | None = None,
    expectation_type: str | None = None,
    config: dict[str, Any] | None = None,
    warn_threshold: Decimal | None = None,
    fail_threshold: Decimal | None = None,
    critical_threshold: Decimal | None = None,
    source_connection_id: uuid.UUID | None = None,
    actor_id: uuid.UUID | None = None,
) -> Check:
    """Partial update, snapshotting the post-update state as a new version (#280).

    Follows the codebase PATCH convention (connections / suites): a `None`
    argument means "not provided", so an omitted field is left unchanged. v1 has
    no clear-to-NULL path for thresholds; recreate the check to drop one. The
    same applies to `source_connection_id` (a comparison check can be repointed,
    never cleared — the kind requires it, ADR 0015).
    """
    check = get_check(session, suite_id, check_id)
    if source_connection_id is not None and check.kind != COMPARISON_KIND:
        raise CheckConfigInvalidError(
            "only comparison checks carry a source connection (ADR 0015)",
            detail={"kind": check.kind, "field": "source_connection_id"},
        )
    # Compute the effective post-patch values and validate them BEFORE touching
    # the ORM object: a rejected update must leave nothing dirty in the session

"""Check CRUD — checks are GX expectations nested under a suite.

A check belongs to exactly one suite (FK + cascade). This layer validates the
suite exists, enforces the v1 monitor-kind limit, and validates the check's
`config` at author time: expectation-kind checks resolve + construct their GX
expectation class (#651 — the same translation the runner performs, pulled
forward so garbage 422s instead of persisting and only failing at run time);
validation against live data remains the dry-run path, not CRUD.

Kind gating (ADR 0012): the schema CHECK reserves `freshness / volume /
schema_drift / anomaly / comparison`; authorable today are `expectation`, the
freshness/volume monitor kinds, and `comparison` (ADR 0015 — source ref +
config validated here; its runner lands with #794, until then it yields an
`error` result). `schema_drift` / `anomaly` remain schema-valid but refused —
authoring one would produce a check that can never execute.

FastAPI-free like the sibling services: takes a `Session`, returns ORM models,
raises `DataQError` subclasses.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import uuid
from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import UTC, datetime, timedelta
from decimal import Decimal
from typing import Any

from sqlalchemy import func, select
from sqlalchemy.exc import IntegrityError
from sqlalchemy.orm import Session, selectinload

from backend.app.core.errors import DataQError

145	            f"name must be 1-{_NAME_MAX_LEN} characters",
146	            detail={"field": "name", "length": len(name), "max": _NAME_MAX_LEN},

def update_check(
    session: Session,
    suite_id: uuid.UUID,
    check_id: uuid.UUID,
    *,
    name: str | None = None,
    expectation_type: str | None = None,
    config: dict[str, Any] | None = None,
    warn_threshold: Decimal | None = None,
    fail_threshold: Decimal | None = None,
    critical_threshold: Decimal | None = None,
    source_connection_id: uuid.UUID | None = None,
    actor_id: uuid.UUID | None = None,
) -> Check:
    """Partial update, snapshotting the post-update state as a new version (#280).

    Follows the codebase PATCH convention (connections / suites): a `None`
    argument means "not provided", so an omitted field is left unchanged. v1 has
    no clear-to-NULL path for thresholds; recreate the check to drop one. The
    same applies to `source_connection_id` (a comparison check can be repointed,
    never cleared — the kind requires it, ADR 0015).
    """
    check = get_check(session, suite_id, check_id)
    if source_connection_id is not None and check.kind != COMPARISON_KIND:
        raise CheckConfigInvalidError(
            "only comparison checks carry a source connection (ADR 0015)",
            detail={"kind": check.kind, "field": "source_connection_id"},
        )
    # Compute the effective post-patch values and validate them BEFORE touching

286	def test_create_check_rejects_nul_bytes(db_session: Any, monkeypatch: Any) -> None:
287	    """NUL can't reach Postgres (#567) — the MCP boundary rejects it as a clean
288	    ToolError (mirroring the REST ApiModel guard), wherever it hides: the name
289	    or a nested config value."""
290	    user = _user(db_session)
291	    suite = _suite(db_session, user)
292	    _as(monkeypatch, db_session, user)
293	
294	    with pytest.raises(ToolError, match="NUL"):
295	        server.create_check(
296	            str(suite.id),
297	            name="evil-\x00-check",
298	            expectation_type="expect_column_values_to_not_be_null",
299	            config={"column": "email"},
300	        )
301	    with pytest.raises(ToolError, match="NUL"):
302	        server.create_check(
303	            str(suite.id),
304	            name="fine",
305	            expectation_type="expect_column_values_to_be_in_set",
306	            config={"column": "status", "value_set": ["ok", "bad\x00value"]},

    ToolError (mirroring the REST ApiModel guard), wherever it hides: the name

input.command:
gh issue create \
  --title "MCP create_check/update_check accept unbounded name/expectation_type (REST enforces 256/128)" \
  --label "bug,security,priority/P3" \
  --milestone "v1.1 Week 3 — Azure wind-down + local-first posture" \
  --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary

Found during the weekly `/security-scan` OWASP spot-check (2026-07-05 → 2026-07-12 batch review).

The MCP `create_check` tool (`backend/app/mcp/server.py`) has no Pydantic validation layer of its own, unlike the REST `CheckCreate`/`CheckUpdate` models (`backend/app/api/v1/checks.py`), which cap `name` at 256 chars and `expectation_type` at 128 chars. `check_service.create_check`/`update_check` themselves enforced no length limit either — so an MCP caller (edit-permission-gated, authenticated) could submit an arbitrarily large `name`/`expectation_type`.

Worse than just "unbounded storage": the `checks` table columns are `String(256)`/`String(128)` (`backend/app/db/models.py`), so an over-length write wasn't silently accepted — it would hit a raw Postgres `StringDataRightTruncation` error on INSERT/UPDATE, an unhandled-exception path rather than a clean 422/ToolError.

## Fix

Pushed the length validation down into `check_service` (`_validate_lengths`, mirroring the REST bounds + column widths) so it's enforced once for every caller — REST (defense in depth, Pydantic still catches it first) and MCP (the actual gap) alike. Added `test_create_check_rejects_oversized_name_or_type` in `backend/tests/mcp/test_mcp_tools.py`.

## Severity

Low — authenticated + edit-permission-gated, no injection or authz bypass, just an input-validation / error-shape inconsistency between the two entry points.

Fixes via same-day PR.
EOF
)" 2>&1

content.content: Launching skill: code-review

content.text:
`high effort → 3+5 angles × 6 candidates → 1-vote verify (recall-biased) → ≤10 findings`

You are reviewing for **recall** at high effort: catch every real bug a careful
reviewer would catch in one sitting. At this level, catching real bugs matters
more than avoiding false positives. Err on the side of surfacing.

## Phase 0 — Gather the diff

Run `git diff @{upstream}...HEAD` (or `git diff main...HEAD` / `git diff HEAD~1`
if there's no upstream) to get the unified diff under review. If there are
uncommitted changes, or the range diff is empty, also run `git diff HEAD` and
include the working-tree changes in scope — the review often runs before the
commit. If a PR number, branch name, or file path was passed as an argument,
review that target instead. Treat this diff as the review scope.

## Phase 1 — Find candidates (3 correctness angles + 3 cleanup angles + 1 altitude angle + 1 conventions angle, up to 6 each)

Run **8 independent finder angles** via the Agent tool. Each
surfaces **up to 6 candidate findings** with `file`, `line`, a one-line
`summary`, and a concrete `failure_scenario`.

### Angle A — line-by-line diff scan

Read every hunk in the diff, line by line. Then Read the enclosing function for
each hunk — bugs in unchanged lines of a touched function are in scope (the PR
re-exposes or fails to fix them). For every line ask: what input, state, timing,
or platform makes this line wrong? Look for inverted/wrong conditions,
off-by-one, null/undefined deref, missing `await`, falsy-zero checks,
wrong-variable copy-paste, error swallowed in catch, unescaped regex metachars.

### Angle B — removed-behavior auditor

For every line the diff DELETES or replaces, name the invariant or behavior it
enforced, then search the new code for where that invariant is re-established.
If you can't find it, that's a candidate: a removed guard, a dropped error
path, a narrowed validation, a deleted test that was covering a real case.

### Angle C — cross-file tracer

For each function the diff changes, find its callers (Grep for the symbol) and
check whether the change breaks any call site: a new precondition, a changed
return shape, a new exception, a timing/ordering dependency. Also check callees:
does a parallel change in the same PR make a call unsafe?

### Reuse

The angles above hunt for bugs; this one and the next two hunt for cleanup in
the changed code. Flag new code that re-implements something the codebase
already has — Grep shared/utility modules and files adjacent to the change,
and name the existing helper to call instead.

### Simplification

Flag unnecessary complexity the diff adds: redundant or derivable state,
copy-paste with slight variation, deep nesting, dead code left behind. Name
the simpler form that does the same job.

### Efficiency

Flag wasted work the diff introduces: redundant computation or repeated I/O,
independent operations run sequentially, blocking work added to startup or
hot paths. Also flag long-lived objects built from closures or captured
environments — they keep the entire enclosing scope alive for the object's
lifetime (a memory leak when that scope holds large values); prefer a
class/struct that copies only the fields it needs. Name the cheaper
alternative.

### Altitude

Check that each change is implemented at the right depth, not as a fragile
bandaid. Special cases layered on shared infrastructure are a sign the fix
isn't deep enough — prefer generalizing the underlying mechanism over adding
special cases.

### Conventions (CLAUDE.md)

Find the CLAUDE.md files that govern the changed code: the user-level
~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, the repo-root CLAUDE.md, plus any CLAUDE.md or
CLAUDE.local.md in a directory that is an ancestor of a changed file (a
directory's CLAUDE.md only applies to files at or below it). Read each one
that exists, then check the diff for clear violations of the rules they state.

Only flag a violation when you can quote the exact rule and the exact line
that breaks it — no style preferences, no vague "spirit of the doc"
inferences. In the finding, name the CLAUDE.md path and quote the rule so the
report can cite it. If no CLAUDE.md applies, return nothing for this angle.

Cleanup, altitude, and conventions candidates use the same
`file`/`line`/`summary` shape; in `failure_scenario`, state the concrete
cost (what is duplicated, wasted, harder to maintain, or which CLAUDE.md rule
is broken) instead of a crash. Correctness bugs always outrank cleanup,
altitude, and conventions findings when the output cap forces a cut.

Pass every candidate with a nameable failure scenario through — finders that
silently drop half-believed candidates bypass the verify step and are the
dominant cause of misses.

## Phase 2 — Verify (1-vote, recall-biased)

Dedup near-duplicates (same defect, same location, same reason → keep one). For
each remaining candidate, run **one verifier** via the Agent tool:
give it the diff, the relevant file(s), and the candidate; it returns exactly
one of **CONFIRMED / PLAUSIBLE / REFUTED**.

**PLAUSIBLE by default** — do not refute a candidate for being "speculative" or
"depends on runtime state" when the state is realistic: concurrency races,
nil/undefined on a rare-but-reachable path (error handler, cold cache, missing
optional field), falsy-zero treated as missing, off-by-one on a boundary the
code does not exclude, retry storms / partial failures, regex/allowlist that
lost an anchor. These are PLAUSIBLE.

**REFUTED** only when constructible from the code: factually wrong (quote the
actual line); provably impossible (type/constant/invariant — show it); already
handled in this diff (cite the guard); or pure style with no observable effect.

Keep **CONFIRMED and PLAUSIBLE**. Drop REFUTED.

## Output

Return findings as a JSON array of at most 10 objects:

```json
[
  {
    "file": "path/to/file.ext",
    "line": 123,
    "summary": "one-sentence statement of the bug",
    "failure_scenario": "concrete inputs/state → wrong output/crash"
  }
]
```

Ranked most-severe first. If more than 10 survive, keep the 10 most
severe. If nothing survives verification, return `[]`.


## Posting to GitHub (--comment)

The `--comment` flag was passed. After producing the findings list, if the
review target is a GitHub PR, post each finding as an inline PR comment via
`mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment` (one call per finding;
include a suggestion block only when it fully fixes the issue). If that tool
is not available in this session, fall back to `gh api` (repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{pr}/comments)
or print the findings instead. If the target is not a PR, print the findings
to the terminal and note that `--comment` was ignored.

content.content:
diff --git a/backend/app/services/check_service.py b/backend/app/services/check_service.py
index 27c08565..bc391e39 100644
--- a/backend/app/services/check_service.py
+++ b/backend/app/services/check_service.py
@@ -130,6 +130,34 @@ def validate_kind(kind: str) -> None:
         )
 
 
+# Mirrors the `checks.name` / `checks.expectation_type` column widths (db/models.py)
+# and the REST `CheckCreate`/`CheckUpdate` Field bounds (api/v1/checks.py). Enforced
+# here too so every caller — including the MCP `create_check`/`update_check` tools,
+# which have no Pydantic layer of their own — gets a clean 422 instead of a raw
+# `StringDataRightTruncation` from Postgres on an over-length INSERT/UPDATE.
+_NAME_MAX_LEN = 256
+_EXPECTATION_TYPE_MAX_LEN = 128
+
+
+def _validate_lengths(*, name: str | None, expectation_type: str | None) -> None:
+    if name is not None and not (1 <= len(name) <= _NAME_MAX_LEN):
+        raise CheckConfigInvalidError(
+            f"name must be 1-{_NAME_MAX_LEN} characters",
+            detail={"field": "name", "length": len(name), "max": _NAME_MAX_LEN},
+        )
+    if expectation_type is not None and not (
+        1 <= len(expectation_type) <= _EXPECTATION_TYPE_MAX_LEN
+    ):
+        raise CheckConfigInvalidError(
+            f"expectation_type must be 1-{_EXPECTATION_TYPE_MAX_LEN} characters",
+            detail={
+                "field": "expectation_type",
+                "length": len(expectation_type),
+                "max": _EXPECTATION_TYPE_MAX_LEN,
+            },
+        )
+
+
 def validate_monitor_check(
     kind: str,
     config: dict[str, Any],
@@ -492,6 +520,7 @@ def create_check(
     """
     suite = get_suite(session, suite_id)  # 404 if the suite is missing
     validate_kind(kind)
+    _validate_lengths(name=name, expectation_type=expectation_type)
     if kind != COMPARISON_KIND and source_connection_id is not None:
         raise CheckConfigInvalidError(
             "only comparison checks carry a source connection (ADR 0015)",
@@ -584,6 +613,7 @@ def update_check(
     never cleared — the kind requires it, ADR 0015).
     """
     check = get_check(session, suite_id, check_id)
+    _validate_lengths(name=name, expectation_type=expectation_type)
     if source_connection_id is not None and check.kind != COMPARISON_KIND:
         raise CheckConfigInvalidError(
             "only comparison checks carry a source connection (ADR 0015)",
diff --git a/backend/tests/mcp/test_mcp_tools.py b/backend/tests/mcp/test_mcp_tools.py
index 1aed4a3d..8929cef6 100644
--- a/backend/tests/mcp/test_mcp_tools.py
+++ b/backend/tests/mcp/test_mcp_tools.py
@@ -307,6 +307,31 @@ def test_create_check_rejects_nul_bytes(db_session: Any, monkeypatch: Any) -> No
         )
 
 
+def test_create_check_rejects_oversized_name_or_type(db_session: Any, monkeypatch: Any) -> None:
+    """The MCP tool has no Pydantic layer of its own, so `name`/`expectation_type`
+    length is enforced by `check_service` — matching the REST `CheckCreate` bounds
+    (256/128) and the `checks` column widths — as a clean ToolError, not a raw
+    Postgres `StringDataRightTruncation` on the INSERT."""
+    user = _user(db_session)
+    suite = _suite(db_session, user)
+    _as(monkeypatch, db_session, user)
+
+    with pytest.raises(ToolError, match="name"):
+        server.create_check(
+            str(suite.id),
+            name="x" * 257,
+            expectation_type="expect_column_values_to_not_be_null",
+            config={"column": "email"},
+        )
+    with pytest.raises(ToolError, match="expectation_type"):
+        server.create_check(
+            str(suite.id),
+            name="fine",
+            expectation_type="x" * 129,
+            config={"column": "email"},
+        )
+
+
 def test_create_check_requires_edit(db_session: Any, monkeypatch: Any) -> None:
     owner = _user(db_session, "owner@acme.io")
     suite = _suite(db_session, owner)

input.description: Simplification and efficiency review
input.prompt:
Code review — Angles: simplification + efficiency + altitude. Repo: /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ, branch fix/issue-813-mcp-check-length-validation (checked out).

Diff: adds `_validate_lengths(*, name: str | None, expectation_type: str | None) -> None` to backend/app/services/check_service.py (two length checks, each raising `CheckConfigInvalidError`), called once each from `create_check` and `update_check`. Also adds one test to backend/tests/mcp/test_mcp_tools.py.

Read the full `_validate_lengths` function, its two call sites in `create_check`/`update_check`, and the surrounding functions in backend/app/services/check_service.py for context.

Task — evaluate for:
1. **Simplification**: any redundant/derivable state, needlessly complex boolean logic (e.g. `not (1 <= len(x) <= MAX)` vs a clearer form), or structure that could be simpler while doing the same job.
2. **Efficiency**: any wasted work — e.g. is `len()` on a potentially huge string (someone could still pass a 10MB string as `name` before hitting this check) itself a cost concern, and would checking length before or after any other operation matter? Is there redundant computation across the two calls in create_check/update_check?
3. **Altitude**: is enforcing this bound in `check_service` (shared by REST defense-in-depth + MCP) the right depth, or is it a bandaid that should instead have been solved by giving the MCP tool decorator/framework its own Pydantic-equivalent validation layer (i.e., is there an existing MCP-wide input-validation mechanism this bypasses, or is service-layer enforcement the established pattern for this codebase's other cross-cutting guards like `_reject_nul`)? Check how `_reject_nul` (grep for its definition and doc comment in backend/app/mcp/server.py) solves an analogous "MCP has no Pydantic" problem, and whether `_validate_lengths` should have been folded into a similar generic MCP-boundary helper instead of the service layer, or whether service-layer is indeed more correct here (multiple callers, not just MCP).

Return up to 6 candidate findings as a JSON array of {file, line, summary, failure_scenario}. failure_scenario should state the concrete cost or the simpler alternative. If none, return [].

```json
[
  {

    "line": 138,
    "summary": "`_NAME_MAX_LEN = 256` / `_EXPECTATION_TYPE_MAX_LEN = 128` are a third independent literal for bounds already declared in `checks.py`'s `CheckCreate`/`CheckUpdate` Field(max_length=...) and `models.py`'s `String(256)`/`String(128)` column widths. No shared constants module exists anywhere in `backend/app/` (confirmed via repo-wide search) for either pair to import from, and there's no test asserting the three stay in sync.",
    "failure_scenario": "If `checks.name`/`checks.expectation_type` column widths are ever widened in a migration (e.g. `String(256)` -> `String(512)`), the developer must remember to update this literal too; missing it silently reopens the exact gap this PR is fixing — but now only for MCP callers, since the REST Pydantic Field would be updated (it's co-located with the route) while this service-layer copy, three files away, is easy to forget."
  },
  {
    "file": "backend/app/services/check_service.py",
    "line": 133,
    "summary": "The comment explicitly says these constants 'mirror' the api/v1/checks.py Field bounds and db/models.py column widths, i.e. the PR author already knew this was a duplicate-literal risk and chose to note it in a comment rather than derive/import it. Given `checks.py` imports `check_service` (not vice versa), importing the constant from the API layer would invert the api->service dependency direction, so the constant genuinely can't be pulled from `checks.py` without restructuring — but deriving it from `Check.name.type.length` (SQLAlchemy column metadata, the single source of truth already sitting in `db/models.py`) was not attempted.",
    "failure_scenario": "A future refactor of the checks table (renaming/splitting the name column, or changing its type) has no compiler/test signal pointing back at this literal; the values quietly drift apart and the service-layer guard either rejects valid input or lets through what the DB will now reject with a 500."
  },
  {
    "file": "backend/app/services/notification_service.py",
    "line": 115,
    "summary": "Established precedent for this exact pattern already exists: `_RECIPIENTS_MAX_LEN = 1024  # matches the suite_notifications.email_recipients column`, a standalone literal in the service layer with a comment pointing at the DB column, no shared-constants import. Unlike the new check_service.py case, there's no competing Pydantic `Field(max_length=...)` for `email_recipients` in `api/v1/notifications.py` (it's a plain `str | None`), so this duplicates a bound that lived in exactly one place (the DB column) rather than being a third copy of a value already replicated twice.",
    "failure_scenario": "This is not a new failure scenario to flag — it's supporting evidence that the codebase's established convention IS 'duplicate the numeric literal across DB schema / Pydantic schema / service-layer guard with an explanatory comment', not 'derive it from one source.' Treating the check_service.py addition as a novel problem worth blocking on would be inconsistent with this prior art; the finding above (drift risk / no shared source) is a valid pre-existing gap in the codebase's convention, not something introduced fresh by this PR."
  }
]
```

Key takeaways for the reviewer:

- No shared-constants module exists in `backend/app/core/` or `backend/app/services/` for length bounds anywhere in the repo — this was verified by grepping for `MAX_LEN`/`max_length` across `services/` and `core/`, and by `find` for any `constants`/`limits` module (none found).
- The one prior example of the same shape — `backend/app/services/notification_service.py:115` (`_RECIPIENTS_MAX_LEN = 1024`) — duplicates a DB column width as a bare literal with a mirroring comment, no shared import. So duplicating the literal with a comment is the established pattern here, not an anomaly `check_service.py` invented.
- The one structural difference worth flagging: in the notification case the literal duplicates a bound that exists in only one other place (the DB column — there's no Pydantic `max_length` for `email_recipients`). In the new check_service.py case, the bound already exists in *two* other places (`checks.py` Field **and** `models.py` column), so this literal is a third copy, not a second. That's a marginally higher drift risk than the precedent, but it's a difference of degree, not a new category of problem for this codebase.
- No generic length-validation helper exists in `backend/app/services/` or `backend/app/core/` that `_validate_lengths` reinvents — `notification_service.assert_valid_recipients` is shaped for email-specific validation (regex + `@`/domain checks), not a generic bounds-checker, so there's nothing being duplicated there beyond the same "duplicate the numeric literal" idiom.
- `checks.py` imports `check_service` (`from backend.app.services import check_service as svc`), not the reverse — so importing the constant from `api/v1/checks.py` into the service layer would invert the intended api→service dependency direction and would itself be a layering smell, which is presumably why the PR author didn't do it.

Given the precedent, I'd frame this as a minor/optional cleanup note rather than a blocking finding — worth a comment suggesting a shared constants source (or deriving from `Check.name.type.length`) if the reviewer wants to reduce the three-way drift risk, but not inconsistent with how the codebase already handles this trade-off elsewhere.</result>

[
  {
    "file": "backend/app/services/suite_io_service.py",
    "line": 207,
    "summary": "import_suite() builds `Check(name=c[\"name\"], expectation_type=c[\"expectation_type\"], ...)` directly as an ORM object — it never calls check_service.create_check(), so the new `_validate_lengths` guard is completely bypassed on the import path.",
    "failure_scenario": "A suite-export document (or a hand-crafted import payload — suite_io_service has no length checks of its own on `name`/`expectation_type`) with a check name >256 chars or expectation_type >128 chars sails through all of import_suite's per-check validation (validate_kind/validate_monitor_check/validate_comparison_check/validate_custom_sql_check/validate_expectation_check — none of which check string length) and only fails at `session.commit()` (line 224) with a raw Postgres StringDataRightTruncation/IntegrityError. There is no handler for SQLAlchemy IntegrityError in backend/app/core/errors.py, so it falls through to `_unhandled_exception_handler` and returns a 500, not the clean 422 the sibling PR was written to guarantee. This is exactly the failure mode `_validate_lengths` was added to eliminate, just not fixed for this caller."
  },
  {
    "file": "backend/app/services/suite_io_service.py",
    "line": 222,
    "summary": "record_check_version() is called for every imported check (mirroring create_check's own call), but since the Check rows above were never length-validated, an over-length name/expectation_type would also be copied verbatim into check_versions — same root cause as the finding above, just visible one line later on the version-snapshot insert instead of the checks insert (whichever table's write executes first inside the flush/commit).",
    "failure_scenario": "Same as above: if the `checks` insert somehow succeeded (e.g. a future column widening) but `check_versions` kept the old width, or vice versa, a partially-successful import could commit checks but 500 on the version snapshot, leaving an inconsistent partially-imported suite despite the module's own atomicity claim (\"a bad document never leaves a half-imported suite behind\")."
  }
]
```

Points 2 and 3 from the task were checked and are **not** findings: (2) `CheckUpdate.name`/`expectation_type` are typed `str | None` with `Field(min_length=1, max_length=256/128)` — verified empirically that Pydantic v2 applies the length constraint only to the `str` union member, so an explicit JSON `null` validates straight to `None` without ever exercising `min_length`, matching the "not provided" sentinel `_validate_lengths` already expects (`if name is not None`). No double-reject, no unexpected behavior. (3) `checks.name`/`checks.expectation_type` are `String(256)`/`String(128)` (`backend/app/db/models.py:383,389`) and `check_versions.name`/`expectation_type` are identically `String(256)`/`String(128)` (lines 441, 443) — both exactly match `_NAME_MAX_LEN`/`_EXPECTATION_TYPE_MAX_LEN`, so no width mismatch for `create_check`/`update_check`'s own callers (REST `checks.py`, MCP `create_check`).</result>

REST doesn't feel this (Pydantic's `Field(max_length=...)` already 422s before the handler runs, so the service-layer copy never engages there) — so the wasted work is specific to the MCP path this feature was written for. Hoisting the check into a boundary helper next to `_reject_nul` (called at server.py:371) would give MCP the same zero-DB-cost fail-fast NUL rejection already has."
  },
  {
    "file": "backend/app/services/check_service.py",
    "line": 138,
    "summary": "The bound literals 256/128 are now independently declared in three places with no shared source of truth: `db/models.py:383,389` (`String(256)`/`String(128)` columns), `api/v1/checks.py:36,40` (`Field(max_length=256/128)`), and now `_NAME_MAX_LEN`/`_EXPECTATION_TYPE_MAX_LEN` here.",
    "failure_scenario": "A future migration widening/narrowing `checks.name` or `checks.expectation_type` (e.g. to 512) requires remembering to update this module and api/v1/checks.py too, or the three enforcement layers silently disagree (DB accepts a length REST/MCP reject, or vice versa). Deriving the constants from the SQLAlchemy column (e.g. `Check.name.type.length`) would keep one source of truth instead of three copies that can drift."
  },
  {
    "file": "backend/app/services/check_service.py",
    "line": 142,
    "summary": "The two branches of `_validate_lengths` are structurally identical (range-check a string, raise `CheckConfigInvalidError` with a `{field, length, max}` detail) but are written out as two copy-pasted `if` blocks rather than a loop over `(field, value, max_len)` tuples.",
    "failure_scenario": "Not a bug today, but the duplication means a third bounded field (plausible given `_reject_oversized_config`/`validate_comparison_check` keep adding new string-shaped guards in this file) would likely be added as a third copy-pasted block instead of one extra tuple in a list — the pattern that already causes the 256/128 magic-number drift noted above."
  },
  {
    "file": "backend/app/services/check_service.py",
    "line": 135,
    "summary": "The comment justifying `_validate_lengths` (mirrored in the new test's docstring at backend/tests/mcp/test_mcp_tools.py:311-313) says it protects \"the MCP `create_check`/`update_check` tools\", but `backend/app/mcp/server.py` registers only 8 `@mcp.tool`s and there is no `update_check` tool today — only `create_check`.",
    "failure_scenario": "A reader trusting the comment could believe there's a live MCP `update_check` surface this closes a gap on, when in fact `update_check`'s length guard currently has zero MCP caller and is pure speculative defense-in-depth against a bound REST's own Pydantic model already enforces before the service is ever called — worth being explicit that it's proactive, not closing an existing hole."
  }
]

<result>```json
[
  {
    "file": "backend/app/services/check_service.py",
    "line": 133-137,
    "summary": "New comment on `_validate_lengths` claims it protects 'every caller — including the MCP `create_check`/`update_check` tools' — but `backend/app/mcp/server.py` has no `update_check` tool at all (only 8 MCP tools exist: list_suites, get_suite_results, get_health_score, get_adf_pipeline_status, trigger_suite_run, get_run_status, create_check, profile_column). The comment overstates coverage for a code path that doesn't exist.",
    "failure_scenario": "A future maintainer reads this comment, assumes an MCP `update_check` tool exists and is guarded, and doesn't add length validation when one is eventually built (or wastes time searching for it during an incident). Documentation drift introduced in this exact diff, not merely pre-existing."
  },
  {
    "file": "backend/app/services/suite_io_service.py",
    "line": 201-219,
    "summary": "`import_suite` builds `Check(...)` ORM objects directly from the imported document's dicts (`c['name']`, `c['expectation_type']`) and never calls `check_service.create_check`/`update_check`, so the new `_validate_lengths` guard is not applied here — even though `import_suite` already reuses several other check_service validators (`validate_kind`, `validate_monitor_check`, `validate_comparison_check`, `validate_expectation_check`) from the same module. Today this is masked because the sole caller, the REST import route (`backend/app/api/v1/suites.py`), validates each check via the `CheckDocument` Pydantic model which independently enforces matching `max_length=256`/`128` before calling in. But the enforcement is duplicated/parallel rather than shared, unlike the fix applied to create_check/update_check.",
    "failure_scenario": "If any future caller invokes `suite_io_service.import_suite` directly with dicts not sourced from `CheckDocument` (e.g. a script, a batch-import job, or a future MCP import tool — exactly the gap this PR closes for create_check/update_check), an oversized name/expectation_type reaches `session.flush()`/`commit()` unvalidated and raises the raw Postgres `StringDataRightTruncation` this PR's own stated goal is to eliminate. Also a latent regression risk if `CheckDocument`'s `max_length` is ever loosened independently of `_NAME_MAX_LEN`/`_EXPECTATION_TYPE_MAX_LEN`, since the two are not the same source of truth despite the comment's claim of mirroring column widths everywhere."
  }
]
```

Investigation also confirmed several things checked but found **not** to be bugs (included for reviewer context, not as findings): (1) DB `String(256)`/`String(128)` (`backend/app/db/models.py:383,389`) and REST `CheckCreate`/`CheckUpdate` `Field(max_length=...)` (`backend/app/api/v1/checks.py:36,40,51,52`) numerically match `_NAME_MAX_LEN`/`_EXPECTATION_TYPE_MAX_LEN` exactly, no off-by-one; (2) the `1 <= len(x) <= MAX` bound logic is correctly non-inverted and the `None`-means-"unchanged" semantics in `update_check` are preserved; (3) `_service_errors()` (`backend/app/mcp/server.py:103-110`) does `raise ToolError(exc.message)` verbatim, so the new tests' `pytest.raises(ToolError, match="name"/"expectation_type")` correctly match with no regex-metacharacter issues; (4) no existing test, seed script (`backend/scripts/demo_data.py`), or caller relies on empty/oversized names succeeding through `check_service`, so no regression there; (5) no "duplicate/clone/copy suite" feature exists that could push a previously-valid name over the new 256-char cap via string concatenation.</result>
<usage><subagent_tokens>82719</subagent_tokens><tool_uses>10</tool_uses><duration_ms>436756</duration_ms></usage>
</task-notification>

    if connection.type in ORCHESTRATION_PROVIDERS:
        # Orchestration providers (ADF/Airflow) are never suite datasources
        # (CLAUDE.md §4) — same guard as create_suite, applied at import time.
        raise SuiteImportConnectionInvalidError(
            "orchestration providers cannot be a suite's datasource; "
            "they trigger suites via trigger bindings",
            detail={"connection_id": str(connection_id), "type": connection.type},
        )
    # Validate every check (kind + custom-SQL / monitor / comparison guardrails)
    # up front so a bad document writes nothing. connection.type is known here,
    # so the datasource-gating + config validation that CRUD applies also applies
    # at import (custom-SQL: ADR 0019; freshness/volume monitors: ADR 0012;
    # comparison source refs: ADR 0015). Comparison source refs travel as
    # `(name, env)` and resolve to a connection id per check (index-aligned with
    # `checks` for the construction below).
    source_ids: list[uuid.UUID | None] = []
    for c in checks:
        validate_kind(c["kind"])
        source_ids.append(
            _resolve_source_connection(session, c) if c["kind"] == COMPARISON_KIND else None
        )
        if c["kind"] in MONITOR_KINDS:
            validate_monitor_check(
                c["kind"],
                c["config"],
                expectation_type=c["expectation_type"],
                connection_type=connection.type,
                fail_threshold=c["fail_threshold"],
                critical_threshold=c["critical_threshold"],
            )
        elif c["kind"] == COMPARISON_KIND:
            validate_comparison_check(
                session,
                config=c["config"],
                expectation_type=c["expectation_type"],
                source_connection_id=source_ids[-1],
                suite_connection_type=connection.type,
            )
        elif is_custom_sql(c["expectation_type"]):
            validate_custom_sql_check(
                expectation_type=c["expectation_type"],
                config=c["config"],
                connection_type=connection.type,
            )
        else:
            # Same author-time GX validation as check CRUD (#651) — an imported
            # document must not smuggle in checks a direct POST would 422.
            validate_expectation_check(c["expectation_type"], c["config"])

    suite = Suite(
        name=name,
        description=description,
        connection_id=connection_id,
        created_by=created_by,
    )
    suite.checks = [
        Check(
            name=c["name"],
            kind=c["kind"],
            expectation_type=c["expectation_type"],
            source_connection_id=source_id,
            config=c["config"],
            warn_threshold=c["warn_threshold"],
            fail_threshold=c["fail_threshold"],
            critical_threshold=c["critical_threshold"],
        )
        for c, source_id in zip(checks, source_ids, strict=True)
    ]

toolUseResult.originalFile:
"""Suite export / import — portable, connection-agnostic suite documents.

A suite export is a plain dict (the API serialises it to JSON): the suite's
`name` / `description` plus every check's authoring fields. It deliberately
**omits** all DB-internal identity — `id`, `connection_id`, `created_by`,
timestamps — so a document is a reusable template, not a row dump. Import binds
the document to a *freshly chosen* connection and creates a new owned suite.

Why connection-agnostic: a suite's checks describe table/column expectations,
not a specific datasource row. Re-binding on import is the whole point (copy a
QA suite onto the UAT connection). The connection is supplied at import time and
validated like `create_suite` (422 on a missing connection).

Import is **atomic**: every check kind is validated *before* any row is written,
then the suite and its checks persist in a single commit — a bad document never
leaves a half-imported suite behind.

FastAPI-free like the sibling services: takes a `Session`, returns ORM models /
dicts, raises `DataQError` subclasses.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import uuid
from typing import Any

from sqlalchemy import select
from sqlalchemy.orm import Session

from backend.app.core.errors import DataQError
from backend.app.core.logging import get_logger
from backend.app.datasources.monitors import MONITOR_KINDS
from backend.app.db.models import COMPARISON_KIND, ORCHESTRATION_PROVIDERS, Check, Connection, Suite
from backend.app.services.check_service import (
    record_check_version,
    validate_comparison_check,
    validate_expectation_check,
    validate_kind,
    validate_monitor_check,
)
from backend.app.services.custom_sql import is_custom_sql, validate_custom_sql_check

log = get_logger(__name__)

# Bump when the document shape changes incompatibly; import refuses unknown
# versions rather than silently misreading an older/newer layout.
EXPORT_VERSION = 1


class SuiteImportInvalidError(DataQError):
    status_code = 422
    code = "suite_import_invalid"


class SuiteImportConnectionInvalidError(DataQError):
    status_code = 422
    code = "suite_import_connection_invalid"


def export_suite(session: Session, suite: Suite) -> dict[str, Any]:
    """Build a portable document from an already-loaded, authorised suite.

    The API resolves and authorises the suite via `require_permission`, then
    passes it here. Checks are emitted in stable creation order so two exports of
    an unchanged suite are byte-identical (diffable in version control).

    A comparison check's source ref (ADR 0015) is serialized portably as the
    connection's `(name, env)` — a raw UUID would never survive a workspace
    move; import resolves it back (or 422s). The key is emitted only for
    comparison checks, so pre-0015 documents and consumers are unaffected.
    """
    checks = sorted(suite.checks, key=lambda c: c.created_at)
    docs: list[dict[str, Any]] = []
    for c in checks:
        doc: dict[str, Any] = {
            "name": c.name,
            "kind": c.kind,
            "expectation_type": c.expectation_type,
            "config": c.config,
            "warn_threshold": c.warn_threshold,
            "fail_threshold": c.fail_threshold,
            "critical_threshold": c.critical_threshold,
        }
        if c.source_connection_id is not None:
            # RESTRICT FK: a referenced source connection cannot have been
            # deleted, so the row always resolves.
            source = session.get(Connection, c.source_connection_id)
            assert source is not None
            doc["source_connection"] = {"name": source.name, "env": source.env}
        docs.append(doc)
    return {
        "version": EXPORT_VERSION,
        "name": suite.name,
        "description": suite.description,
        "checks": docs,
    }


def _resolve_source_connection(session: Session, check_doc: dict[str, Any]) -> uuid.UUID:
    """Resolve a comparison check's portable source ref to a connection id.

    The document carries `source_connection: {"name", "env"}` (ADR 0015);
    `(name, env)` is unique (`uq_connections_name_env`), so at most one row
    matches. Missing key or no match → 422 naming the check, so a document
    exported elsewhere fails imports with an actionable error, not a stray FK.
    """
    ref = check_doc.get("source_connection")
    if not isinstance(ref, dict) or not ref.get("name") or not ref.get("env"):
        raise SuiteImportInvalidError(
            "a comparison check needs source_connection {name, env} in the document",
            detail={"check": check_doc.get("name")},
        )
    source_id = session.scalar(
        select(Connection.id).where(Connection.name == ref["name"], Connection.env == ref["env"])
    )
    if source_id is None:
        raise SuiteImportInvalidError(
            "comparison source connection not found on this workspace — create it "
            "(same name and env) before importing",
            detail={"check": check_doc.get("name"), "source_connection": ref},
        )
    return source_id


def import_suite(
    session: Session,
    *,
    version: int,
    name: str,
    description: str | None,
    checks: list[dict[str, Any]],
    connection_id: uuid.UUID,
    created_by: uuid.UUID,
) -> Suite:
    """Create a new suite + checks from a document, bound to `connection_id`.

    Raises `SuiteImportInvalidError` (422) for an unsupported document version or
    an unsupported check kind, and `SuiteImportConnectionInvalidError` (422) if
    the target connection does not exist. Atomic: validates everything before
    writing, then commits the suite and all checks together.
    """
    if version != EXPORT_VERSION:
        raise SuiteImportInvalidError(
            f"unsupported export version {version!r}; this server imports v{EXPORT_VERSION}",
            detail={"version": version, "supported": EXPORT_VERSION},
        )
    connection = session.get(Connection, connection_id)
    if connection is None:
        raise SuiteImportConnectionInvalidError(
            "connection not found", detail={"connection_id": str(connection_id)}
        )
    if connection.type in ORCHESTRATION_PROVIDERS:
        # Orchestration providers (ADF/Airflow) are never suite datasources
        # (CLAUDE.md §4) — same guard as create_suite, applied at import time.
        raise SuiteImportConnectionInvalidError(
            "orchestration providers cannot be a suite's datasource; "
            "they trigger suites via trigger bindings",
            detail={"connection_id": str(connection_id), "type": connection.type},
        )
    # Validate every check (kind + custom-SQL / monitor / comparison guardrails)
    # up front so a bad document writes nothing. connection.type is known here,
    # so the datasource-gating + config validation that CRUD applies also applies
    # at import (custom-SQL: ADR 0019; freshness/volume monitors: ADR 0012;
    # comparison source refs: ADR 0015). Comparison source refs travel as
    # `(name, env)` and resolve to a connection id per check (index-aligned with
    # `checks` for the construction below).
    source_ids: list[uuid.UUID | None] = []
    for c in checks:
        validate_kind(c["kind"])
        source_ids.append(
            _resolve_source_connection(session, c) if c["kind"] == COMPARISON_KIND else None
        )
        if c["kind"] in MONITOR_KINDS:
            validate_monitor_check(
                c["kind"],
                c["config"],
                expectation_type=c["expectation_type"],
                connection_type=connection.type,
                fail_threshold=c["fail_threshold"],
                critical_threshold=c["critical_threshold"],
            )
        elif c["kind"] == COMPARISON_KIND:
            validate_comparison_check(
                session,
                config=c["config"],
                expectation_type=c["expectation_type"],
                source_connection_id=source_ids[-1],
                suite_connection_type=connection.type,
            )
        elif is_custom_sql(c["expectation_type"]):
            validate_custom_sql_check(
                expectation_type=c["expectation_type"],
                config=c["config"],
                connection_type=connection.type,
            )
        else:
            # Same author-time GX validation as check CRUD (#651) — an imported
            # document must not smuggle in checks a direct POST would 422.
            validate_expectation_check(c["expectation_type"], c["config"])

    suite = Suite(
        name=name,
        description=description,
        connection_id=connection_id,
        created_by=created_by,
    )
    suite.checks = [
        Check(
            name=c["name"],
            kind=c["kind"],
            expectation_type=c["expectation_type"],
            source_connection_id=source_id,
            config=c["config"],
            warn_threshold=c["warn_threshold"],
            fail_threshold=c["fail_threshold"],
            critical_threshold=c["critical_threshold"],
        )
        for c, source_id in zip(checks, source_ids, strict=True)
    ]
    session.add(suite)
    session.flush()  # assign check ids so each can carry a v1 snapshot (#280)
    for check in suite.checks:
        record_check_version(session, check, actor_id=created_by)
    session.commit()
    session.refresh(suite)
    log.info(
        "suite_imported",
        suite_id=str(suite.id),
        connection_id=str(connection_id),
        check_count=len(checks),
    )
    return suite
